title: Why the Odd Numbers Add to Perfect Squares

md: Add up the odd numbers, in order, and watch the running total:
Every total is a **perfect square**: $1, 4, 9, 16, 25, \ldots$ That is not a coincidence — and one picture proves it.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: The sum of the first $n$ odd numbers is exactly $n^2$:  $\;1+3+5+\cdots+(2n-1) = n^2.$

md: ### Proof 1 — Build the square in L-shaped layers
Start with a single square — that's $1$. To grow it into the next-bigger square, you wrap an **L-shaped layer** around two sides. How many squares does that L hold? Always the next **odd** number.

A $1\times1$ square has $1$ cell. Wrapping it into a $2\times2$ needs $3$ more. Wrapping *that* into a $3\times3$ needs $5$ more. Each L-shaped layer is one cell longer on each arm than the last — so it adds $2$ each time, marching through the odd numbers.
Drag the slider: each colored L is an odd number, and together they tile a perfect $n \times n$ square.
Since $n$ layers fill an $n \times n$ square, the layers must add to $n^2$ — that's what the square *is*.
### Proof 2 — Algebra, using the Gauss sum
The $k$-th odd number is $2k - 1$. So the sum we want is:
We already know $1+2+\cdots+n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ (see the Gauss-sum proof). Substitute it in:

blocks.tex: 2\cdot\frac{n(n+1)}{2} - n = n(n+1) - n = n^2 + n - n = n^2

md: The algebra and the picture agree.

blocks.title: A bonus pattern
blocks.body: This explains a classic trick: the gap between consecutive squares is always odd. $7^2 - 6^2 = 13$, and $13$ is exactly the L-shaped layer you'd add to a $6\times6$ square to reach $7\times7$.
